channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.description:
1. NASA's Psyche mission delivers Mars flyby data, time-lapse video  Phys.org

  3. JPL Releases Psyche Images And Video Of Mars  Orbital Today
  4. NASA’s Psyche mission tests LLNL-built gamma-ray sensor on Mars flyby | Newswise  Newswise
  5. NASA’s Psyche Mission Captures Crescents and Surface Details During Recent Martian Flyby  The Debrief

item.title: 'It's absolutely exciting!': Astronomers discover an atmosphere around an Earth-like planet in the habitable zone for first time ever - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'It's absolutely exciting!': Astronomers discover an atmosphere around an Earth-like planet in the habitable zone for first time ever  Live Science
  2. Astronomers Find an Atmosphere on a Nearby Earthlike Planet  The New York Times
  3. Earth-like exoplanet found to have an atmosphere  The Guardian
  4. First atmosphere detected on a habitable-zone rocky world  Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian
  5. First atmosphere found on Earth-like planet in habitable zone of distant star  BBC

  3. A sweet discovery: Astronomers say sugar is lurking in the space between stars  PBS
  4. A sugar key to the origins of life has been discovered in interstellar space  CNN
  5. A Sweet Surprise: Scientists Find Sugar Deep in Our Galaxy  The New York Times

item.description:
1. Meteorite that hit New Jersey home may unlock clues to life’s origins  FOX 8 News

  4. Meteorite that crashed into New Jersey home contains "alien world chemistry," scientists say  CBS News

item.description:

  4. Can Plants Make Dairy Protein Without Cows? New Study Comes Close  StudyFinds

item.title: The lines etched into Voyager’s Golden Record cover point towards 14 pulsars whose spinning clocks reveal both where the Sun is and roughly when the spacecraft left — directions designed to work without relying on a single human language - Space Daily
item.description: The lines etched into Voyager’s Golden Record cover point towards 14 pulsars whose spinning clocks reveal both where the Sun is and roughly when the spacecraft left — directions designed to work without relying on a single human language  Space Daily

item.title: Across Titan’s equator, winds have built dunes around 100 metres high and hundreds of kilometres long — but the grains are water ice coated in hydrocarbons that fell out of the moon’s orange atmosphere - Space Daily
item.description: Across Titan’s equator, winds have built dunes around 100 metres high and hundreds of kilometres long — but the grains are water ice coated in hydrocarbons that fell out of the moon’s orange atmosphere  Space Daily

item.title: The Sahara turns green roughly every 20,000 years, and the last time it did, hippos and crocodiles lived in lakes where there is now only sand - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Sahara turns green roughly every 20,000 years, and the last time it did, hippos and crocodiles lived in lakes where there is now only sand  Space Daily
  2. We tend to think of the Sahara as the world's great permanent desert, but researchers have found it was a green savanna with hippos, giraffes, and rivers as recently as 6,000 years ago — and that it collapsed into desert within one to two centuries, a tipping po  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: A Major Ocean Current Is On The Verge of Collapse. Scientists Say The Effects May Reach California - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. A Major Ocean Current Is On The Verge of Collapse. Scientists Say The Effects May Reach California  ScienceAlert
  2. After record heat, could the Atlantic make Britain's weather even more extreme?  BBC
  3. How a hidden shift in the Atlantic could rewire the seasons  The Times of India
  4. Citizens Addressing our Changing Climate: Shifts in ocean currents due to climate change  Bemidji Pioneer
  5. What Cold Water In The North Atlantic Means For Hurricane Season  SURFER Magazine

item.title: In 1980, Voyager 1 flew close enough to Titan to reveal its thick nitrogen atmosphere — but the gravity-assist geometry bent the spacecraft permanently out of the planetary plane, trading Uranus and Neptune for one close look at Saturn’s largest moon - Space Daily
item.description: In 1980, Voyager 1 flew close enough to Titan to reveal its thick nitrogen atmosphere — but the gravity-assist geometry bent the spacecraft permanently out of the planetary plane, trading Uranus and Neptune for one close look at Saturn’s largest moon  Space Daily

item.description:
1. 1,000 Days Until ‘God Of Chaos’ Asteroid Stuns Skywatchers  Forbes

  3. These 5 asteroids are passing closer to Earth than you might expect  starlust.org
  4. Asteroid Apophis' 2029 flyby of Earth will be visible to billions of people  The Weather Network

item.title: Geologists have taught for decades that rivers needed plants to form their distinctive S-shaped meanders, but Stanford University researchers published a study in August 2025 showing that rivers were carving those curves long before vegetation existed on la - Space Daily
item.description: Geologists have taught for decades that rivers needed plants to form their distinctive S-shaped meanders, but Stanford University researchers published a study in August 2025 showing that rivers were carving those curves long before vegetation existed on la  Space Daily

item.title: When elephants play, they rapidly copy one another’s trunk and head movements — a form of bodily mimicry resembling the contagious smiles and laughter seen in humans - Space Daily
item.description: When elephants play, they rapidly copy one another’s trunk and head movements — a form of bodily mimicry resembling the contagious smiles and laughter seen in humans  Space Daily

item.description:

  2. ‘How 20-minute workouts helped me transform my body at 40'  Women's Health

item.title: Mercury is shrinking — as its iron core slowly cools, the whole planet has contracted by up to seven kilometres, wrinkling its surface into cliffs hundreds of miles long - Space Daily
item.description: Mercury is shrinking — as its iron core slowly cools, the whole planet has contracted by up to seven kilometres, wrinkling its surface into cliffs hundreds of miles long  Space Daily

item.title: Enceladus, a Saturn moon just 500 kilometres wide, fires geysers of water thousands of kilometres into space through cracks at its south pole — and that water carries the building blocks of life - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Enceladus, a Saturn moon just 500 kilometres wide, fires geysers of water thousands of kilometres into space through cracks at its south pole — and that water carries the building blocks of life  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s moon Enceladus is spraying a hidden ocean into space — and inside that spray is the same chemical fuel that feeds life in Earth’s deepest oceans  Space Daily

item.description:
1. Can We Ever Understand Consciousness?  The Dispatch

item.title: Scientists identify the rare meteorite that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago - ScienceDaily
item.description:
1. Scientists identify the rare meteorite that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago  ScienceDaily

  3. Dinosaurs May Have Been Wiped Out by ‘Oddball’ Space Rock  Sci.News
  4. The Dinosaur Extinction Was More Complicated Than You Think  New Scientist
  5. Scientists identify rare meteorite class that killed dinosaurs  The Independent

item.title: In 2004 a NASA probe caught a comet's tail on a sheet of aerogel, flew the grains 4.6 billion kilometres home, and dropped them into the Utah desert — the first solid pieces of a comet ever returned to Earth - Space Daily
item.description: In 2004 a NASA probe caught a comet's tail on a sheet of aerogel, flew the grains 4.6 billion kilometres home, and dropped them into the Utah desert — the first solid pieces of a comet ever returned to Earth  Space Daily

item.title: An AI searched nearly 100 million old Hubble images in two and a half days and found 1,300 cosmic oddities — more than 800 of them had never appeared in the scientific literature - Space Daily
item.description: An AI searched nearly 100 million old Hubble images in two and a half days and found 1,300 cosmic oddities — more than 800 of them had never appeared in the scientific literature  Space Daily

item.title: This New Process Turns Plastic Trash Into Clean Fuel Instead of Pollution - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. This New Process Turns Plastic Trash Into Clean Fuel Instead of Pollution  Gizmodo
  2. Chemical treatment slashes emissions of making hydrogen from plastic waste  Chemistry World
  3. New catalyst could make mixed plastic waste recyclable in one chemical step  Phys.org
  4. Single-reactor process yields 90% pure hydrogen fuel from mixed plastic waste  Interesting Engineering
  5. New Process Turns Mixed Plastic Waste Directly Into Hydrogen Fuel  Design and Development Today

item.title: Astronauts on the ISS watch 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets a day, and many say the hardest thing to describe back home is how thin the atmosphere looks from above — a bright blue line you could almost rub out with a thumb - Space Daily
item.description: Astronauts on the ISS watch 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets a day, and many say the hardest thing to describe back home is how thin the atmosphere looks from above — a bright blue line you could almost rub out with a thumb  Space Daily

item.title: A primitive dwarf galaxy with almost none of the heavy elements found in the Milky Way is somehow producing iron and silicon-carbide dust — materials astronomers thought early galaxies would struggle to make - Space Daily
item.description: A primitive dwarf galaxy with almost none of the heavy elements found in the Milky Way is somehow producing iron and silicon-carbide dust — materials astronomers thought early galaxies would struggle to make  Space Daily

item.description: Nanoparticles could remove harmful immune molecules from blood  Phys.org

item.title: Researchers extracted water directly from lunar soil in July 2025 and used sunlight to convert it into breathable oxygen and rocket fuel — cutting out any need to ship water from Earth, where a single gallon costs an estimated $83,000 to launch - Space Daily
item.description: Researchers extracted water directly from lunar soil in July 2025 and used sunlight to convert it into breathable oxygen and rocket fuel — cutting out any need to ship water from Earth, where a single gallon costs an estimated $83,000 to launch  Space Daily

item.description: Minnesota’s giant water bugs are big, bizarre and surprisingly helpful  Star Tribune

item.title: Six miles below the Pacific, with no sunlight and no photosynthesis, a Chinese submersible found thousands of blood-red tubeworms and white clams thriving on leaking methane — the deepest animal ecosystem ever discovered on Earth - Space Daily
item.description: Six miles below the Pacific, with no sunlight and no photosynthesis, a Chinese submersible found thousands of blood-red tubeworms and white clams thriving on leaking methane — the deepest animal ecosystem ever discovered on Earth  Space Daily

item.title: A planet orbiting twin suns was directly photographed in 2025, hugging its two stars six times closer than any other world ever found in a binary system — a real version of Tatooine that should not, according to current theory, be able to exist there - Space Daily
item.description: A planet orbiting twin suns was directly photographed in 2025, hugging its two stars six times closer than any other world ever found in a binary system — a real version of Tatooine that should not, according to current theory, be able to exist there  Space Daily

item.description: A Giant Structure Is Towering Over The Milky Way. After 40 Years, Scientists Have Worked Out What It Is  ScienceAlert

item.title: New Black Hole Theory Solves a 50-Year-Old Problem - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. New Black Hole Theory Solves a 50-Year-Old Problem  SciTechDaily
  2. Stephen Hawking's famous 'leaky' black hole theory gets much-needed update  Space
  3. 'Smaller than the tiniest scale in nature': Physicists made a black hole out of light and used it to test Stephen Hawking's elusive radiation theory  Live Science
  4. Hawking Radiation Recoil Confirmed in Lab, Revealing Simpler Black Hole Mechanism  Tech Times
  5. Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade  ScienceDaily

item.description: Spend A Day As A Sea Bunny And We’ll Tell You Which Ocean You Belong In  BuzzFeed

item.title: Scientists use relay synthesis to create key building blocks of reserve antibiotic to combat resistance - Phys.org
item.description: Scientists use relay synthesis to create key building blocks of reserve antibiotic to combat resistance  Phys.org

item.title: Voyager 1 has no engine running. The 38,000-mile-per-hour speed it has held since its Saturn flyby in 1980 is borrowed momentum from planets it grazed more than four decades ago, and that free ride carries it to one light-day from Earth this November - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 has no engine running. The 38,000-mile-per-hour speed it has held since its Saturn flyby in 1980 is borrowed momentum from planets it grazed more than four decades ago, and that free ride carries it to one light-day from Earth this November  Space Daily
  2. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. NASA's Voyager 1 Only Has Two Active Science Instruments Left After Latest System Shutdown  Hypebeast
  5. ATLANTIC SKIES: How a spacecraft launched in 1977 is sending signals beyond our solar system  PNI Atlantic News

item.description: If a whale gets lost, can it find its way home? The kids’ quiz  The Guardian

item.title: Prehistoric insects were absolutely massive. Here's why they needed to be so darn big - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description: Prehistoric insects were absolutely massive. Here's why they needed to be so darn big  BBC Wildlife Magazine

item.description: We've just realised our understanding of the universe might be wrong  Metro.co.uk
